speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4. December 7, 1995. We're speaking with Dr. Emil Arnold. Dr. Arnold, you were discussing that you were discharged from the Polish Army. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, shortly after the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 13 years old. And what happened next? You-- you mentioned that you were living in [? Lodz, ?] that you reunited with your mother and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother and sister, uh, left Lutsk and went to the western part of Poland. They, uh, had an apartment in Lodz. My mother, uh, had  a job with the Polish government. Uh, and, uh, my sister was going to school. And, uh, I joined them after I was discharged from the army. I started going to high school. And, uh, we started thinking about leaving Poland and going to the US. Mother want to join her sister in New York. It was, uh, very difficult to, uh, go directly from Poland to, uh, to the US-- almost impossible. So the only way, uh, was to somehow leave Poland, go to the-- to Germany, to the American, uh, occupation zone in Germany, and try to arrange to emigrate to the US from there. So we left Poland, which wasn't really-- didn't turn out to be as difficult as-- as it seems. There was a concerted effort on-- on-- [INAUDIBLE] on-- on the part of the Zionist movement to get all the Jews who were left out of Poland and, uh, bring them to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh, they, uh, managed to bribe the border guards to let these people go through the border out of Poland. To Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from Poland, the way it worked in practice was to go to Czechoslovakia and enter Austria. Somehow, that-- that-- that was the route. Those people who escaped were not only the Holocaust survivors, because people who were deported by the Russians originally in 1939 and 1940 to Siberia started returning to Poland. So there were a number of people-- people who survived the German occupation and people who came from Russia trying to escape from Poland. Some Jews remained in Poland. But, uh, many tried to get out. So what we did was to move to the border town near, uh-- near the Czech border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first we went to [? Wroclaw, ?] which is a big town, and then a little village, I don't remember the name, near the, uh, Czech border. The town on Czech that I remember is called Nachod. So we, uh, went through there. Uh, the guards let everybody go through. But, uh, somehow, it was organized that they could get small groups of people-- groups of 10 or 12 every hour or so. So we just waited for a signal and then went through. I remember the British tried to stop this, because they-- they-- they knew that most of these people were really eventually going to wind up in Israel. And they were trying to stop that at that time. So that-- that was in 1946-- about middle of 1946. So, uh, I remember seeing a, uh-- a limousine, a black limousine, with a Polish and a British flag driving back and forth to the border and then and back, trying somehow to, uh, make sure that nobody crosses the border. I imagine it didn't do much good, because everybody got through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to walk a short distance with our belongings through the border. The border guards just wave-- waved us through. There were-- there was a train waiting on the other side-- freight cars. We boarded the freight cars. The freight cars were sealed. And somehow, the train could go through the Russian-Austrian border. And it wind up in eventually in Salzburg. We stopped for a short time in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Uncle Carl and Susan and [? Bella, ?] who also survived the Holocaust, they, uh, got out of Poland. And, uh, they were in Vienna. So we were in Vienna overnight during the transport. And we managed to reunite with them just for a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you were crossing the borders and on that freight car, did you notice other peop-- other Jewish people who were survivors of camps? Or are these people who had other ways of surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I imagined there were some-- people who were hiding, people who were hiding in the woods, people were in concentration camps too. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, going back to when you were in high school for a month in Lodz, did you notice if any of the people who were in the high school were concentration camp victims?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no Jews in that particular high school. And as far as everybody else was concerned, I was not Jewish. I even remember going to church one Sunday with some of my colleagues. I didn't fell-- feel very comfortable about that. But, uh they didn't-- they didn't ask many questions. I never actually remember specifically saying I was not Jewish. But it was just assumed. There were not many Jews around. So it just was assumed that I was not Jewish, especially since they knew that I had just got of the Polish Army. So, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this that you went to church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, well nearby church. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz. During my high school, uh, time in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So proceeding with your journey from Czechoslovakia, you were then in Vienna. And you reunited with your cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- yes. So we stayed overnight in Vienna and train went on. And then we were for, uh, several months in Salzburg in a DP-- Displaced Persons camp-- in Salzburg. It was a temporary relocation camp. From there, we were redirected to Bavaria in a town called Ansbach and Camp Bleidorn. Uh, Camp Bleidorn was formerly a German military, uh, headquarters. So there were large buildings where soldiers and their families, I suppose, lived. Uh, and that was exclusively a Jewish DP camp. It was pretty well organized. Uh, there were a lot of American assistants from, uh, the, uh, various Jewish organizations and American United Nations-- UNRRA, I believe it's called, a refugee organization. So, uh, they provided food and clothing and shelter. And we stayed in that camp until 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a long time-- two years almost-- in that camp. There wasn't very much to do. But, uh, my mother worked in the, uh-- in the uh, uh, camp government. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In administration. I, uh-- not v-- not v-- not-- not very much. I attended-- there were courses organized by ORT, Organization for Rehabilitation through Training. Uh, I, uh-- I took a course and got a certificate in radio repair. I was interested in electronics at the time. So there, I learned about radio. It was actually a pretty good course. Instructors were all Germans. The-- the-- the instruction was done in German. But they were specialists and, uh, did a very good job, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they were not German Jews. They were-- they were Germans, period. So, uh, yeah, there were a lot of Germans working in the camp and, uh, performing all kinds of functions. They were the instructors of these different courses. I remember there was electricians course and a photography course and-- and this radio, uh, technology course. And the instructors were all German. They did a very good job. They were all specialists, and-- and the course was very well taught. There was an examination at the end of the course, and I got a certificate. So that was one thing we did. Then there was Jewish, uh, military training. The Haganah tried to get as many young people back to Israel. And in the meantime, they, uh, sent their representatives, and they taught us, uh, self-defense and, uh, uh, military, uh, training, various kinds. No real weapons, but we learned how to fight with knives and with sticks and that kind of thing. So that was done on a regular basis. And it was pretty much compulsory. They-- they-- they-- they made sure that everybody, uh, attended that course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made sure? Who made sure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, whoever, uh, was in charge of the-- of the, uh, Jewish affairs inside the camp made sure that that was done. And that-- there was a lot of pressure for all the young people to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we didn't want to go to Israel. We wanted to go to the US. And, uh, so we just let them know, we don't want to go to Israel. Nevertheless, they tried to persuade us. Don't you want to go to Israel? Well, my, uh, aunt in New York, uh, arranged for an affidavit to get us, uh, sent to the US. But we were under Polish quota, not Jewish quota. But in those days, immigration was such that we were under Polish quota. Didn't matter that you were Jewish and went through the Holocaust. Still in Polish quota, and the waiting time was five years or 10 years, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the chance of going to the US were very slim, uh, at that time. Things improved later. But at that time, it was very difficult. Well, it so happened, we have family also in Halifax, Canada. So they tried to bring us to Canada, which was easier. The quotas were better? Were the quotas better in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, well, apparently, they were better. So they-- they made the arrangements, and they were convinced that we could just go through the formalities and go there. So we were called to go to the Canadian consulate in a town nearby-- in Munich, I believe. So we went there. Uh, in the meantime, I, uh-- I learned some English. And my mother spoke English fairly well. So we could communicate. So, you know, we talked. That would certainly help in talking to these people. But the Canadian official, who had to sign the papers before he OK'd it, was very hostile. And he said to my mother, look, you don't really want to go to Canada. The papers officially said our cousin in Halifax was a physician. And he said he wants to bring my mother to assist him as a nurse because she got-- she got experience in nursing assisting my father. So that was part of the-- of the affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, look, you don't really want to go to Canada. I know you want to go to the US. You've got a family in the US. You just want to go to Canada because it's easy now. It's no good. I won't let you go. And he said no. So it took a while for our relatives in Halifax to pull some strings. And he-- they finally arranged to get us to, uh-- to Canada. So we went to a reloc-- a relocation center. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was February of 1949. And, uh, got loaded on a plane and wound up in Halifax in February of 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where had you and your mother learned English? You said you knew English pretty well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't much-- my-- my mother knew English before. I guess she always had this idea that she would wind up in America one of these days. She studied English. My father also knew some English. So she could-- she-- she could-- she could speak it fairly well. I took English during the short time in Lodz when I was in high school. I took English. And then during the time in the DP camp, I studied English fairly intensively.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time I finished the, uh-- my time at the DP camp, I already could communicate in English, perhaps not-- not completely, but I could get by in English. So by the time we got to Halifax, at least  I could understand and to make-- make myself understood. Uh, my mother-- we stayed with our cousins for a while, then my mother got an apartment in, uh-- nearby.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to high school. I-- I thought I'd wait a month before I started going to school because I really got very little schooling. And at that time, I already was of high school age. So, uh, you know, I had all this big gap. The-- the real big gap was-- it's mainly mathematics because other things like history and literature, you can always read a book and you don't have to any special background. But mathematics was a big stumbling block. So I picked up an algebra book. And I studied for two weeks, and that was good enough. So I went to, uh-- to high school and I did pretty well in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What grade did you enter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was grade 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: At-- at what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was grade 10. So that was 1949. So I guess I was, uh, 17. So it was just about the right, uh, age. So I guess they put me in grade 10. There were discussions. What should they do with me? They couldn't put me in grade six. So, uh, I guess they decided, well, let's take a chance. So they accepted me in grade 10, you know, just to see how it work-- works out. So I managed pretty well. Uh, I went to high school, finished high school, started college. By that time, we got the permit to go to the US. So my mother and sister went to New York. I stayed behind to finish college. I stayed with my cousins until 1954-- finished college there. I finished college and joined my, uh-- my mother and sister in New York. I went to graduate school at Yale. And, uh, finished, uh-- finished my PhD degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother found work when she was in Halifax?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked in a store, yes, as a-- as a clerk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she came to America, she, uh-- she-- she worked yes, part of the time. She worked for the textile industry as a, uh-- as a, uh, expediter in a transport office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she never-- she never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to America, um, what did you study at Yale?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, chemical physics. I stu-- I studied chemistry in undergraduate school at Dalhousie University in Halifax and chemical physics at Yale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you work now as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work as a physicist in electronics industry. Do research.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two daughters. The older one, Elizabeth, was born in 1969. Uh, she, uh, went to college at Vassar, did pretty well, and then got a master's degree from Princeton, uh, in chemistry. And now she works as a chemist in-- in Princeton. My younger daughter just graduated from University of California at Santa Cruz in American literature. And, uh, she still hasn't decided what to do with herself. She has a job at this point assistant teaching in Santa Cruz. She wants to stay in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you came to America, um, did people ask you about your experiences-- those who may have known that you were a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, people ask. And I-- I don't volunteer the information usually. In fact, I-- I never do. But when people ask me how did you survive, when they-- when they know, I do tell them. Uh, I got married in 1965. My wife is a native Californian. Uh, she, uh-- she had a rough idea what I went through, but I never discuss it in any detail. She didn't push me on that. So, uh-- and I never discussed it with-- with her or with my children really in detail. They-- they only have a rough idea of what I went through. Uh, about a year after I got married, my wife decided she wants to see for herself where, uh-- where I grew up. And she insisted that we go back and take a look. I didn't want to do that. Uh, I didn't see any reason for going back. But she-- she convinced me, persuaded me, that we should go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to a travel agent who specialized in travel to-- to Russia. That was 1966, so it was still in the height of the communist regime. And, uh, that was during Vietnam time. So the relations between, uh, USSR and US were not very friendly and warm. But, uh, we could manage to, uh-- to travel there. We decided to go by car. That was the first year when they allowed, uh, foreigners to actually drive through, uh, Russian territory. So you had to-- to fill out exact itinerary where you're going to be during your stay and pre-pay all the hotels and meals in advance. And this travel agent specialized in this. So he made the arrangements.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to go Lutsk. That was the main purpose of the trip. So he made something for his [INAUDIBLE]. No, he said. No good. You cannot go to Lutsk. That's out of bounds for foreigners. He said, well, maybe when you get to Rovno-- which is the town near Lutsk-- and that was permitted. That's one of the main routes or to Kiev. You can go  to some of the authorities. Maybe you can get permission there. But there's no way you can get permission at this point. So I said, OK, well, uh, I thought I try when we got there. Well, what happened, we bought a car-- a Saab-- had it delivered in Paris. So we flew to Paris, uh, took delivery of the car, made a little side trip, went drove through Italy and through Austria, and then toward Czechoslovakia, to Poland. We went to Krakow. And then, uh, crossed the border into, uh, Ukraine, which was part of Russia then on the way to Rovno. So we were following the prescribed route. We were followed all the time. At first, they didn't make it very obvious. But later on, it became apparent that they were really following us, that they knew where we were every minute. Uh, the route was-- the-- the route was really prearranged. They had these nice whi-- whitewashed homes along the road, and slogans, and placards. Along the road, propaganda signs everywhere with pictures of Castro and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were driving toward Rovno. And all the signs were in both Russian alphabet and Latin alphabet so make sure people know where they're going. Well, along the  road, I saw a sign in Russian only, not-- not-- Latin alphabet, to Lutsk. It was 10 kilometers. So I just-- without thinking-- I made a left turn. And  I started going towards Lutsk. About 30 seconds later, I hear a siren behind me. There's a policeman on a motorcycle. He says, where are you going? Well, it's a good  thing I could communicate with him. I can imagine that, uh, it would've been very difficult if I had another language. I told him, I'm going on to Rovno. So he said, oh, no, you're not. You-- you-- you-- you knew there was a sign there saying Rovno was the other way. You shouldn't have done this. You made a big mistake. Now, we have to hold you up. So he took, uh, our passports and, uh, made some telephone calls. They-- they were very nice about it. He made some small talk in the meantime while they're waiting for instructions. Finally, he came back and said, OK, you can go. Just make sure you stay where you're supposed to next time. So you returned--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we never made-- so we never made Lutsk. So we went on our trip. We went to Kiev, went on to Odessa on the Black Sea. And then we had little problem. Our car broke down. Uh, they filled, uh, the premium gas that was kept in cans. Apparently, the cans were contaminated. The, uh, paint or whatever was in the cans clogged up the entire gas line, got into the engine, and the car could not move.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finally had to ship the car back to the factory in Sweden, the Saab factory in Sweden. And we had to fly to Moscow to make arrangements to have the car shipped. The car was shipped to See-- shipped to Sweden. We finally got it back in the US some months later. But we had to change our itinerary and then flew from Moscow to Sweden and, uh, got back. But I never got to see Lutsk. I, uh-- I think that the-- one of these days, I think we'll go back just to see. So if people don't know, you don't volunteer. But if they ask you, you-- you share your experiences? But do you think it's changed the way you have raised your children, the fact that you're a survivor of the Holocaust? Do you think it affected you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it affected me. Uh, uh, the-- the main effect, I think, is that I'm not willing to really get, uh, close to anybody. So I don't discuss my past with my children. They-- they have only a rough idea what, uh-- of what I went through. So I suppose this is a good opportunity for them. Someday, they will see the tapes. So it will be easier this way, uh, that they can see it instead of me telling them. But I think one of the main effects of the, uh-- of this is that I, uh-- I just find it very difficult to get close to, uh-- to people. And I think it affect-- that definitely affects my relations with my children and family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have-- since the war, have you had dreams? Reoccurring dreams? About--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have. Fortunately, not very often. Uh, yeah. I-- I have had nightmares. On several occasions, they were very, very, scary-- Germans chasing me and things like this, getting caught. I wake up just at the right time. And when I woke up, I-- I would say to myself, well, things weren't really that bad. But, uh, it doesn't happen very often. And, uh, it hasn't happened in-- in a long time. So maybe I'm over that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the-- in the dream, was it Germans chasing you right before you went into hiding? Do you remember what incident may have prompted it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're not related to any real events. It's just a nightmare, uh, without great detail. It's not the details in the dream. It's just the feeling that it evokes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Arnold, could you tell us what this represents?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's, uh, medical diploma from medical school in Prague, Czechoslovakia. Since Jews were not allowed to study medicine in Poland, he had to, uh, study in Czechoslovakia. When we escaped from the ghetto, he, uh, took this out with him and left it with the farm family where we were hiding. And this is how, uh, we came to have it here. We never saw my father, uh, again. But we have his diploma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did he receive this diploma?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe the date on it's 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my grandparents-- my mother's parents-- Marko Schecter and Rachel Schecter. The picture was taken some time during 1930s. They both died before, uh, the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents. And I, uh-- I was, I suppose, about one year old in-- in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents, my sister, and I. My sister is, uh, about four years younger than I am. This is my, uh, sister Eugenie and me and the maid nurse, Vera. The picture was taken about two years before the war, about 1937. This is my mother, my sister, and I in Lutsk shortly before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, about seven-- six, perhaps.
